items.q: Is the primary key always the first column of a table?
items.correct: No - the primary key is defined by its uniqueness property, not its position; it can be any field that uniquely identifies each record.
items.misconception: Yes - whatever column comes first automatically becomes the key.
items.random_wrong: Yes - tables refuse to save unless the key is in column one.
items.why: Examples usually draw the ID first, creating a positional rule in students' minds. Position is presentation; the defining property is guaranteed uniqueness.

items.q: Are a record and a field the same thing?
items.correct: No - a record is one row (everything about one item), while a field is one column (one category of data across all items).
items.misconception: Yes - record and field both mean a box in the table.
items.random_wrong: A record is a column and a field is a row.
items.why: Rows and columns swap easily in memory. Anchor with an example: 'DateOfBirth' is a field; 'all the data about Aisha' is a record.

items.q: Would using one big table for customers and their orders be simpler and better?
items.correct: No - repeating customer details on every order duplicates data, wasting space and causing inconsistencies; linked tables store each fact once.
items.misconception: Yes - one table is always simpler, so it is always better.
items.random_wrong: Yes - relational databases are only used for very small data.
items.why: Single-table thinking feels natural from spreadsheets. The cost appears when a customer's address changes and must be corrected in many rows - the core argument for relational design.

items.q: Which field would make the best primary key for a customer table?
items.why: It's A unique CustomerID number. “The customer's surname” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: A field in a database table is:
items.correct: One column holding a single category of data
items.random_wrong: A backup copy of the table
items.why: It's One column holding a single category of data. “One complete row of data” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.correct: Find and filter records that match chosen criteria
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Find and filter records that match chosen criteria. “Physically repair the disk” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Compared with a flat-file database, a relational database:
items.correct: Stores data in multiple linked tables, reducing duplicated data
items.misconception: Stores everything in one giant table
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Stores data in multiple linked tables, reducing duplicated data. “Stores everything in one giant table” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: A table has 6 fields and 25 records. How many individual data values does it hold?
items.why: Exact answer: 150 values. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
